\section{Week 4: Adaptive Control}\label{week-4-adaptive-control}}

Lyapunov-Based Adaptive Control for Robotic Systems with Parametric Uncertainty

\subsection{Section 0: Week 3 Recall --- Model Predictive Control --- Exam Practice}\label{section-0-week-3-recall-model-predictive-control-exam-practice}}

Before we begin new material, spend 20 minutes working through these two exam-style questions that cover last week\textquotesingle s MPC content. Write your solutions on paper.

\paragraph{Question 1 --- MPC Problem Formulation \& Lifted Form (10 marks)}\label{question-1-mpc-problem-formulation-lifted-form-10-marks}}

Consider a discrete-time linear time-invariant system:

\textbackslash{[}
x\_\{k+1\} = A\textbackslash,x\_k + B\textbackslash,u\_k
\textbackslash{]}

with state \textbackslash(x\_k \textbackslash in \textbackslash mathbb\{R\}\^{}n\textbackslash), control \textbackslash(u\_k \textbackslash in \textbackslash mathbb\{R\}\^{}m\textbackslash), and a finite prediction horizon \textbackslash(N\textbackslash). The MPC cost function is:

\textbackslash{[}
J = \textbackslash sum\_\{i=0\}\^{}\{N-1\}\textbackslash Big{[}\textbackslash big(x\_\{k+i\} - x\_\{\textbackslash mathrm\{ref\}\}\textbackslash big)\^{}T Q \textbackslash big(x\_\{k+i\} - x\_\{\textbackslash mathrm\{ref\}\}\textbackslash big) + u\_\{k+i\}\^{}T R\textbackslash, u\_\{k+i\}\textbackslash Big{]}
\textbackslash{]}

where \textbackslash(Q \textbackslash succeq 0\textbackslash) and \textbackslash(R \textbackslash succ 0\textbackslash).

\textbf{(a)} By recursive substitution, show that the predicted state sequence can be written in compact (lifted) form as:

\textbackslash{[}
\textbackslash mathbf\{X\} = \textbackslash mathcal\{A\}\textbackslash,x\_k + \textbackslash mathcal\{B\}\textbackslash,\textbackslash mathbf\{U\}
\textbackslash{]}

where the stacked vectors are \textbackslash(\textbackslash mathbf\{X\} = \textbackslash begin\{bmatrix\} x\_\{k+1\} \textbackslash\textbackslash{} x\_\{k+2\} \textbackslash\textbackslash{} \textbackslash vdots \textbackslash\textbackslash{} x\_\{k+N\} \textbackslash end\{bmatrix\}\textbackslash) and \textbackslash(\textbackslash mathbf\{U\} = \textbackslash begin\{bmatrix\} u\_k \textbackslash\textbackslash{} u\_\{k+1\} \textbackslash\textbackslash{} \textbackslash vdots \textbackslash\textbackslash{} u\_\{k+N-1\} \textbackslash end\{bmatrix\}\textbackslash), and derive the explicit forms:

\textbackslash{[}
\textbackslash mathcal\{A\} = \textbackslash begin\{bmatrix\} A \textbackslash\textbackslash{} A\^{}2 \textbackslash\textbackslash{} A\^{}3 \textbackslash\textbackslash{} \textbackslash vdots \textbackslash\textbackslash{} A\^{}N \textbackslash end\{bmatrix\}, \textbackslash qquad
\textbackslash mathcal\{B\} = \textbackslash begin\{bmatrix\}
B \& 0 \& 0 \& \textbackslash cdots \& 0 \textbackslash\textbackslash{}
AB \& B \& 0 \& \textbackslash cdots \& 0 \textbackslash\textbackslash{}
A\^{}2B \& AB \& B \& \textbackslash cdots \& 0 \textbackslash\textbackslash{}
\textbackslash vdots \& \textbackslash vdots \& \textbackslash vdots \& \textbackslash ddots \& \textbackslash vdots \textbackslash\textbackslash{}
A\^{}\{N-1\}B \& A\^{}\{N-2\}B \& A\^{}\{N-3\}B \& \textbackslash cdots \& B
\textbackslash end\{bmatrix\}
\textbackslash{]}

Show every substitution step from \textbackslash(i=1\textbackslash) to \textbackslash(i=N\textbackslash).

\textbf{(b)} Identify the structure of \textbackslash(\textbackslash mathcal\{B\}\textbackslash) (lower-triangular block Toeplitz). Explain why this structure arises from the causal nature of the system (future states depend only on past and present inputs).

\paragraph{Question 2 --- Deriving the Optimal MPC Control Law (10 marks)}\label{question-2-deriving-the-optimal-mpc-control-law-10-marks}}

Starting from the lifted quadratic cost:

\textbackslash{[}
J = \textbackslash big(\textbackslash mathbf\{X\} - \textbackslash mathbf\{X\}\_\{\textbackslash mathrm\{ref\}\}\textbackslash big)\^{}T \textbackslash bar\{Q\}\textbackslash,\textbackslash big(\textbackslash mathbf\{X\} - \textbackslash mathbf\{X\}\_\{\textbackslash mathrm\{ref\}\}\textbackslash big) + \textbackslash mathbf\{U\}\^{}T \textbackslash bar\{R\}\textbackslash,\textbackslash mathbf\{U\}
\textbackslash{]}

where \textbackslash(\textbackslash bar\{Q\} = \textbackslash mathrm\{blkdiag\}(Q, Q, \textbackslash dots, Q) \textbackslash in \textbackslash mathbb\{R\}\^{}\{nN \textbackslash times nN\}\textbackslash) and \textbackslash(\textbackslash bar\{R\} = \textbackslash mathrm\{blkdiag\}(R, R, \textbackslash dots, R) \textbackslash in \textbackslash mathbb\{R\}\^{}\{mN \textbackslash times mN\}\textbackslash):

\textbf{(a)} Substitute \textbackslash(\textbackslash mathbf\{X\} = \textbackslash mathcal\{A\}\textbackslash,x\_k + \textbackslash mathcal\{B\}\textbackslash,\textbackslash mathbf\{U\}\textbackslash) into \textbackslash(J\textbackslash) and expand fully:

\textbackslash{[}
J = \textbackslash big(\textbackslash mathcal\{A\}\textbackslash,x\_k + \textbackslash mathcal\{B\}\textbackslash,\textbackslash mathbf\{U\} - \textbackslash mathbf\{X\}\_\{\textbackslash mathrm\{ref\}\}\textbackslash big)\^{}T \textbackslash bar\{Q\}\textbackslash,\textbackslash big(\textbackslash mathcal\{A\}\textbackslash,x\_k + \textbackslash mathcal\{B\}\textbackslash,\textbackslash mathbf\{U\} - \textbackslash mathbf\{X\}\_\{\textbackslash mathrm\{ref\}\}\textbackslash big) + \textbackslash mathbf\{U\}\^{}T \textbackslash bar\{R\}\textbackslash,\textbackslash mathbf\{U\}
\textbackslash{]}

Let \textbackslash(\textbackslash mathbf\{E\} = \textbackslash mathbf\{X\}\_\{\textbackslash mathrm\{ref\}\} - \textbackslash mathcal\{A\}\textbackslash,x\_k\textbackslash). Show that:

\textbackslash{[}
J = \textbackslash mathbf\{U\}\^{}T\textbackslash!\textbackslash big(\textbackslash mathcal\{B\}\^{}T \textbackslash bar\{Q\}\textbackslash,\textbackslash mathcal\{B\} + \textbackslash bar\{R\}\textbackslash big)\textbackslash mathbf\{U\} - 2\textbackslash,\textbackslash mathbf\{E\}\^{}T \textbackslash bar\{Q\}\textbackslash,\textbackslash mathcal\{B\}\textbackslash,\textbackslash mathbf\{U\} + \textbackslash mathbf\{E\}\^{}T \textbackslash bar\{Q\}\textbackslash,\textbackslash mathbf\{E\}
\textbackslash{]}

\textbf{(b)} Take \textbackslash(\textbackslash frac\{\textbackslash partial J\}\{\textbackslash partial \textbackslash mathbf\{U\}\} = 0\textbackslash) and derive:

\textbackslash{[}
\textbackslash mathbf\{U\}\^{}* = \textbackslash big(\textbackslash mathcal\{B\}\^{}T \textbackslash bar\{Q\}\textbackslash,\textbackslash mathcal\{B\} + \textbackslash bar\{R\}\textbackslash big)\^{}\{-1\} \textbackslash mathcal\{B\}\^{}T \textbackslash bar\{Q\}\textbackslash,\textbackslash big(\textbackslash mathbf\{X\}\_\{\textbackslash mathrm\{ref\}\} - \textbackslash mathcal\{A\}\textbackslash,x\_k\textbackslash big)
\textbackslash{]}

\textbf{(c)} Define the MPC gain. Since only the first \textbackslash(m\textbackslash) rows of \textbackslash(\textbackslash mathbf\{U\}\^{}*\textbackslash) are applied (receding horizon), let \textbackslash(E\_1 = {[}I\_m \textbackslash;\textbackslash; 0 \textbackslash;\textbackslash; \textbackslash cdots \textbackslash;\textbackslash; 0{]}\textbackslash). Then:

\textbackslash{[}
u\_k\^{}* = E\_1 \textbackslash mathbf\{U\}\^{}* = \textbackslash underbrace\{E\_1 \textbackslash big(\textbackslash mathcal\{B\}\^{}T \textbackslash bar\{Q\}\textbackslash,\textbackslash mathcal\{B\} + \textbackslash bar\{R\}\textbackslash big)\^{}\{-1\} \textbackslash mathcal\{B\}\^{}T \textbackslash bar\{Q\}\}\_\{K\_\{\textbackslash mathrm\{MPC,ref\}\}\}\textbackslash,\textbackslash mathbf\{X\}\_\{\textbackslash mathrm\{ref\}\} - \textbackslash underbrace\{E\_1 \textbackslash big(\textbackslash mathcal\{B\}\^{}T \textbackslash bar\{Q\}\textbackslash,\textbackslash mathcal\{B\} + \textbackslash bar\{R\}\textbackslash big)\^{}\{-1\} \textbackslash mathcal\{B\}\^{}T \textbackslash bar\{Q\}\textbackslash,\textbackslash mathcal\{A\}\}\_\{K\_\{\textbackslash mathrm\{MPC\}\}\}\textbackslash,x\_k
\textbackslash{]}

Explain why \textbackslash(K\_\{\textbackslash mathrm\{MPC\}\}\textbackslash) is a state-feedback gain and how increasing \textbackslash(R\textbackslash) relative to \textbackslash(Q\textbackslash) affects the control aggressiveness.

\textbf{Time limit: 20 minutes.} This is exactly the format and difficulty level you will encounter in the final exam. Write full derivations showing every step. Partial credit is awarded for correct intermediate work.

\subsection{Section 1: Learning Outcomes}\label{section-1-learning-outcomes}}

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  Understand why adaptive control is needed when system parameters are unknown or time-varying, and articulate the limitations of computed torque and MPC that assume known models.
\item
  Derive the adaptive control law using Lyapunov-based stability analysis, including the construction of a candidate Lyapunov function and verification that its time derivative is negative semi-definite.
\item
  Apply the linearity-in-parameters property of rigid-body dynamics: \textbackslash(M(q)\textbackslash ddot\{q\} + C(q,\textbackslash dot\{q\})\textbackslash dot\{q\} + G(q) = Y(q, \textbackslash dot\{q\}, \textbackslash ddot\{q\})\textbackslash,\textbackslash theta\textbackslash), identifying the regressor matrix \textbackslash(Y\textbackslash) and parameter vector \textbackslash(\textbackslash theta\textbackslash) for specific systems.
\item
  Implement adaptive control for three robotic platforms: a 2-DOF manipulator, a differential-drive mobile robot, and a quadrotor UAV.
\item
  Simulate and compare the performance of fixed-model computed torque control versus adaptive control under parameter uncertainty, demonstrating convergence of parameter estimates.
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Section 3: Part 1 --- Adaptive Control Theory}\label{section-3-part-1-adaptive-control-theory}}

\subsubsection{3.1 Why Adaptive Control?}\label{why-adaptive-control}}

In Week 1, we designed a computed torque controller for a 2-DOF manipulator. The control law was:

\textbackslash{[}
\textbackslash tau = M(q)\textbackslash big(\textbackslash ddot\{q\}\_d + K\_v\textbackslash dot\{e\} + K\_p e\textbackslash big) + C(q,\textbackslash dot\{q\})\textbackslash dot\{q\} + G(q)
\textbackslash{]}

This requires \textbf{exact knowledge} of the inertia matrix \textbackslash(M(q)\textbackslash), Coriolis/centrifugal matrix \textbackslash(C(q,\textbackslash dot\{q\})\textbackslash), and gravity vector \textbackslash(G(q)\textbackslash). In practice, this assumption fails for several reasons:

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  \textbf{Unknown payloads:} A manipulator picks up objects of unknown mass. The effective \textbackslash(m\_2\textbackslash) changes.
\item
  \textbf{Manufacturing tolerances:} Link lengths, masses, and centres of gravity are only approximately known.
\item
  \textbf{Wear and degradation:} Friction coefficients, gear ratios, and joint stiffness change over time.
\item
  \textbf{Model simplification:} We ignore link flexibility, actuator dynamics, and higher-order effects.
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Key insight:} If the model parameters used in computed torque are wrong, the nonlinear cancellation is imperfect, and the closed-loop system is no longer a simple linear error system. Tracking performance degrades, and stability may be lost.

Adaptive control addresses this by estimating the unknown parameters online, while simultaneously controlling the system. The parameter estimates converge to values that ensure good tracking, even if the true parameters are never exactly identified.

\textbf{Figure 1:} Adaptive control block diagram. The {blue control loop} tracks the reference, while the {orange adaptation loop} estimates unknown plant parameters and feeds them to the controller.

\textbf{Figure 2:} Comparison of {Direct} and {Indirect} adaptive control. In direct adaptation, the error signal is fed to an adaptation law that adjusts controller gains K(t) directly. In indirect adaptation, the error drives a parameter estimator to obtain θ̂, which is then used in a gain computation step to determine K(θ̂).

\subsubsection{3.2 The Linearity-in-Parameters Property}\label{the-linearity-in-parameters-property}}

The dynamics of a rigid-body robotic system can always be written as:

\textbackslash( M(q)\textbackslash ddot\{q\} + C(q,\textbackslash dot\{q\})\textbackslash dot\{q\} + G(q) = Y(q, \textbackslash dot\{q\}, \textbackslash ddot\{q\})\textbackslash,\textbackslash theta \textbackslash)

where:

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  \textbackslash(Y(q, \textbackslash dot\{q\}, \textbackslash ddot\{q\}) \textbackslash in \textbackslash mathbb\{R\}\^{}\{n \textbackslash times p\}\textbackslash) is the \textbf{regressor matrix} --- a known matrix that depends on joint positions, velocities, and accelerations, but \emph{not} on the unknown parameters.
\item
  \textbackslash(\textbackslash theta \textbackslash in \textbackslash mathbb\{R\}\^{}p\textbackslash) is the \textbf{parameter vector} --- a constant vector containing all the unknown dynamic parameters (products of masses, lengths, inertias, friction coefficients, etc.).
\end{itemize}

This is the \emph{linearity-in-parameters} property. It holds because the equations of motion are derived from the Lagrangian, which is quadratic in velocities and linear in the inertial parameters. The property is fundamental to adaptive control: it allows us to separate what we know (the structure of \textbackslash(Y\textbackslash)) from what we do not know (the values in \textbackslash(\textbackslash theta\textbackslash)).

\textbf{Figure 3:} The linearity-in-parameters property separates robot dynamics into a {known regressor matrix Y} (computable from kinematics) multiplied by an {unknown parameter vector θ} (to be estimated online).

\subsubsection{3.3 Concrete Example: 2-DOF Planar Manipulator}\label{concrete-example-2-dof-planar-manipulator}}

\textbf{Figure 4:} 2-DOF planar manipulator. {Link 1} (length l₁, mass m₁) and {Link 2} (length l₂, mass m₂) rotate at joints with angles θ₁ and θ₂. Joint torques τ₁ and τ₂ are the control inputs.

Consider the 2-DOF planar manipulator with link masses \textbackslash(m\_1, m\_2\textbackslash), link lengths \textbackslash(l\_1, l\_2\textbackslash), and gravity \textbackslash(g\textbackslash). The standard Euler-Lagrange dynamics are:

\textbackslash{[}
M(q)\textbackslash ddot\{q\} + C(q,\textbackslash dot\{q\})\textbackslash dot\{q\} + G(q) = \textbackslash tau
\textbackslash{]}

with:

\textbackslash{[}
M(q) = \textbackslash begin\{bmatrix\}
m\_1 l\_1\^{}2 + m\_2(l\_1\^{}2 + l\_2\^{}2 + 2l\_1 l\_2 \textbackslash cos q\_2) \& m\_2(l\_2\^{}2 + l\_1 l\_2 \textbackslash cos q\_2) \textbackslash\textbackslash{}
m\_2(l\_2\^{}2 + l\_1 l\_2 \textbackslash cos q\_2) \& m\_2 l\_2\^{}2
\textbackslash end\{bmatrix\}
\textbackslash{]}
\textbackslash{[}
C(q,\textbackslash dot\{q\}) = \textbackslash begin\{bmatrix\}
-m\_2 l\_1 l\_2 \textbackslash sin(q\_2)\textbackslash,\textbackslash dot\{q\}\_2 \& -m\_2 l\_1 l\_2 \textbackslash sin(q\_2)(\textbackslash dot\{q\}\_1 + \textbackslash dot\{q\}\_2) \textbackslash\textbackslash{}
m\_2 l\_1 l\_2 \textbackslash sin(q\_2)\textbackslash,\textbackslash dot\{q\}\_1 \& 0
\textbackslash end\{bmatrix\}
\textbackslash{]}
\textbackslash{[}
G(q) = \textbackslash begin\{bmatrix\}
(m\_1 + m\_2)\textbackslash,g\textbackslash,l\_1 \textbackslash sin q\_1 + m\_2\textbackslash,g\textbackslash,l\_2 \textbackslash sin(q\_1 + q\_2) \textbackslash\textbackslash{}
m\_2\textbackslash,g\textbackslash,l\_2 \textbackslash sin(q\_1 + q\_2)
\textbackslash end\{bmatrix\}
\textbackslash{]}

Define the parameter vector:

\textbackslash{[}
\textbackslash theta = \textbackslash begin\{bmatrix\} \textbackslash theta\_1 \textbackslash\textbackslash{} \textbackslash theta\_2 \textbackslash\textbackslash{} \textbackslash theta\_3 \textbackslash\textbackslash{} \textbackslash theta\_4 \textbackslash\textbackslash{} \textbackslash theta\_5 \textbackslash\textbackslash{} \textbackslash theta\_6 \textbackslash end\{bmatrix\}
= \textbackslash begin\{bmatrix\} m\_1 l\_1\^{}2 \textbackslash\textbackslash{} m\_2 l\_1\^{}2 \textbackslash\textbackslash{} m\_2 l\_2\^{}2 \textbackslash\textbackslash{} m\_2 l\_1 l\_2 \textbackslash\textbackslash{} (m\_1 + m\_2)\textbackslash,g\textbackslash,l\_1 \textbackslash\textbackslash{} m\_2\textbackslash,g\textbackslash,l\_2 \textbackslash end\{bmatrix\}
\textbackslash{]}

Then the regressor matrix \textbackslash(Y \textbackslash in \textbackslash mathbb\{R\}\^{}\{2 \textbackslash times 6\}\textbackslash) such that \textbackslash(\textbackslash tau = Y(q, \textbackslash dot\{q\}, \textbackslash ddot\{q\})\textbackslash,\textbackslash theta\textbackslash) is:

\textbackslash{[}
Y = \textbackslash begin\{bmatrix\}
\textbackslash ddot\{q\}\_1 \& \textbackslash ddot\{q\}\_1 \& \textbackslash ddot\{q\}\_1 + \textbackslash ddot\{q\}\_2 \& 2\textbackslash cos(q\_2)\textbackslash ddot\{q\}\_1 + \textbackslash cos(q\_2)\textbackslash ddot\{q\}\_2 - \textbackslash sin(q\_2)\textbackslash dot\{q\}\_2\textbackslash dot\{q\}\_1 - \textbackslash sin(q\_2)(\textbackslash dot\{q\}\_1+\textbackslash dot\{q\}\_2)\textbackslash dot\{q\}\_2 \& \textbackslash sin(q\_1) \& \textbackslash sin(q\_1+q\_2) \textbackslash\textbackslash{}
0 \& 0 \& \textbackslash ddot\{q\}\_1 + \textbackslash ddot\{q\}\_2 \& \textbackslash cos(q\_2)\textbackslash ddot\{q\}\_1 + \textbackslash sin(q\_2)\textbackslash dot\{q\}\_1\^{}2 \& 0 \& \textbackslash sin(q\_1+q\_2)
\textbackslash end\{bmatrix\}
\textbackslash{]}

You can verify: multiply \textbackslash(Y\textbackslash theta\textbackslash) and recover exactly \textbackslash(M(q)\textbackslash ddot\{q\} + C(q,\textbackslash dot\{q\})\textbackslash dot\{q\} + G(q)\textbackslash). Every entry of \textbackslash(Y\textbackslash) is computable from measured (or commanded) \textbackslash(q, \textbackslash dot\{q\}, \textbackslash ddot\{q\}\textbackslash).

\subsubsection{3.4 The Adaptive Control Law}\label{the-adaptive-control-law}}

We now construct the adaptive controller. Define the tracking error and its filtered version:

\textbackslash{[}
e = q\_d - q, \textbackslash qquad \textbackslash dot\{e\} = \textbackslash dot\{q\}\_d - \textbackslash dot\{q\}
\textbackslash{]}

Define the \textbf{sliding variable} (composite error):

\textbackslash{[}
s = \textbackslash dot\{e\} + \textbackslash Lambda\textbackslash, e
\textbackslash{]}

where \textbackslash(\textbackslash Lambda = \textbackslash Lambda\^{}T \textbackslash succ 0\textbackslash) is a positive definite design matrix. Equivalently, define the \textbf{reference velocity} and \textbf{reference acceleration}:

\textbackslash{[}
\textbackslash dot\{q\}\_r = \textbackslash dot\{q\}\_d + \textbackslash Lambda\textbackslash,e = \textbackslash dot\{q\} + s
\textbackslash{]}
\textbackslash{[}
\textbackslash ddot\{q\}\_r = \textbackslash ddot\{q\}\_d + \textbackslash Lambda\textbackslash,\textbackslash dot\{e\}
\textbackslash{]}

Notice that \textbackslash(s = \textbackslash dot\{q\}\_r - \textbackslash dot\{q\}\textbackslash), so \textbackslash(s \textbackslash to 0\textbackslash) implies \textbackslash(\textbackslash dot\{q\} \textbackslash to \textbackslash dot\{q\}\_r\textbackslash), which combined with the definition of \textbackslash(\textbackslash dot\{q\}\_r\textbackslash) implies both \textbackslash(e \textbackslash to 0\textbackslash) and \textbackslash(\textbackslash dot\{e\} \textbackslash to 0\textbackslash) exponentially (since \textbackslash(\textbackslash dot\{e\} + \textbackslash Lambda e = 0\textbackslash) is a stable linear ODE).

The \textbf{adaptive control law} is:

\textbackslash( \textbackslash tau = Y(q, \textbackslash dot\{q\}, \textbackslash dot\{q\}\_r, \textbackslash ddot\{q\}\_r)\textbackslash,\textbackslash hat\{\textbackslash theta\} + K\_D\textbackslash, s \textbackslash)

where:

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  \textbackslash(\textbackslash hat\{\textbackslash theta\}\textbackslash) is the current estimate of the parameter vector \textbackslash(\textbackslash theta\textbackslash).
\item
  \textbackslash(Y(q, \textbackslash dot\{q\}, \textbackslash dot\{q\}\_r, \textbackslash ddot\{q\}\_r)\textbackslash) is the regressor evaluated at the \emph{reference} velocity and acceleration (not the actual \textbackslash(\textbackslash ddot\{q\}\textbackslash), which we cannot measure cleanly).
\item
  \textbackslash(K\_D = K\_D\^{}T \textbackslash succ 0\textbackslash) is a positive definite gain matrix providing damping on the sliding variable.
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Important subtlety:} We evaluate the regressor at \textbackslash((\textbackslash dot\{q\}\_r, \textbackslash ddot\{q\}\_r)\textbackslash) instead of \textbackslash((\textbackslash dot\{q\}, \textbackslash ddot\{q\})\textbackslash). This avoids the need to measure or numerically differentiate joint accelerations, and it ensures that the regressor is known (computable from \textbackslash(q, \textbackslash dot\{q\}, q\_d, \textbackslash dot\{q\}\_d, \textbackslash ddot\{q\}\_d\textbackslash)).

\subsubsection{3.5 Parameter Update Law}\label{parameter-update-law}}

The parameter estimates are updated according to:

\textbackslash( \textbackslash dot\{\textbackslash hat\{\textbackslash theta\}\} = \textbackslash Gamma\^{}\{-1\}\textbackslash, Y\^{}T\textbackslash! s \textbackslash)

where \textbackslash(\textbackslash Gamma = \textbackslash Gamma\^{}T \textbackslash succ 0\textbackslash) is the \textbf{adaptation gain matrix}. Define the parameter estimation error:

\textbackslash{[}
\textbackslash tilde\{\textbackslash theta\} = \textbackslash hat\{\textbackslash theta\} - \textbackslash theta
\textbackslash{]}

Since \textbackslash(\textbackslash theta\textbackslash) is constant, \textbackslash(\textbackslash dot\{\textbackslash tilde\{\textbackslash theta\}\} = \textbackslash dot\{\textbackslash hat\{\textbackslash theta\}\}\textbackslash).

\textbf{Figure 8:} Parameter convergence under adaptive control. Multiple parameter estimates starting from different initial conditions all converge toward the true parameter value θ* (dashed line). The adaptation law drives estimates toward values that minimize tracking error.

\subsubsection{3.6 Lyapunov Stability Proof}\label{lyapunov-stability-proof}}

\paragraph{Theorem (Adaptive Control Stability)}\label{theorem-adaptive-control-stability}}

Under the adaptive control law \textbackslash(\textbackslash tau = Y\textbackslash hat\{\textbackslash theta\} + K\_D s\textbackslash) with the update law \textbackslash(\textbackslash dot\{\textbackslash hat\{\textbackslash theta\}\} = \textbackslash Gamma\^{}\{-1\} Y\^{}T s\textbackslash), the sliding variable \textbackslash(s \textbackslash to 0\textbackslash) and consequently the tracking errors \textbackslash(e \textbackslash to 0\textbackslash), \textbackslash(\textbackslash dot\{e\} \textbackslash to 0\textbackslash) as \textbackslash(t \textbackslash to \textbackslash infty\textbackslash).

\textbf{Figure 7:} Lyapunov stability concept. Left: the Lyapunov function V forms a bowl-shaped surface, and the system trajectory (pink spiral) descends toward the equilibrium. Right: V(t) monotonically decreases over time since V̇ = -sᵀK₄s is always non-positive.

\textbf{Proof.} Define the candidate Lyapunov function:

\textbackslash{[}
V = \textbackslash frac\{1\}\{2\}\textbackslash,s\^{}T M(q)\textbackslash,s + \textbackslash frac\{1\}\{2\}\textbackslash,\textbackslash tilde\{\textbackslash theta\}\^{}T \textbackslash Gamma\textbackslash,\textbackslash tilde\{\textbackslash theta\}
\textbackslash{]}

Since \textbackslash(M(q) \textbackslash succ 0\textbackslash) and \textbackslash(\textbackslash Gamma \textbackslash succ 0\textbackslash), both terms are strictly positive for \textbackslash((s, \textbackslash tilde\{\textbackslash theta\}) \textbackslash neq (0,0)\textbackslash), so \textbackslash(V\textbackslash) is a valid positive definite candidate with \textbackslash(V = 0\textbackslash) iff \textbackslash(s = 0\textbackslash) and \textbackslash(\textbackslash tilde\{\textbackslash theta\} = 0\textbackslash).

\textbf{Step 1: Time derivative of \textbackslash(V\textbackslash).}

\textbackslash{[}
\textbackslash dot\{V\} = s\^{}T M(q)\textbackslash,\textbackslash dot\{s\} + \textbackslash frac\{1\}\{2\}\textbackslash,s\^{}T \textbackslash dot\{M\}(q)\textbackslash,s + \textbackslash tilde\{\textbackslash theta\}\^{}T \textbackslash Gamma\textbackslash,\textbackslash dot\{\textbackslash tilde\{\textbackslash theta\}\}
\textbackslash{]}

\textbf{Step 2: Simplify \textbackslash(s\^{}T M\textbackslash dot\{s\} + \textbackslash tfrac\{1\}\{2\}s\^{}T\textbackslash dot\{M\}s\textbackslash) using skew-symmetry.}\\
The Euler-Lagrange formulation guarantees that \textbackslash(N := \textbackslash dot\{M\} - 2C\textbackslash) is skew-symmetric, i.e.\textbackslash{} \textbackslash(s\^{}T N s = 0\textbackslash) for all \textbackslash(s\textbackslash). This gives \textbackslash(\textbackslash tfrac\{1\}\{2\}s\^{}T\textbackslash dot\{M\}s = s\^{}T C s\textbackslash), so:
\textbackslash{[}
s\^{}T M\textbackslash dot\{s\} + \textbackslash tfrac\{1\}\{2\}s\^{}T\textbackslash dot\{M\}s = s\^{}T(M\textbackslash dot\{s\} + Cs)
\textbackslash{]}

\textbf{Step 3: Evaluate \textbackslash(M\textbackslash dot\{s\} + Cs\textbackslash).}\\
Since \textbackslash(s = \textbackslash dot\{q\}\_r - \textbackslash dot\{q\}\textbackslash), we have \textbackslash(\textbackslash dot\{s\} = \textbackslash ddot\{q\}\_r - \textbackslash ddot\{q\}\textbackslash). Substituting \textbackslash(M\textbackslash ddot\{q\} = \textbackslash tau - C\textbackslash dot\{q\} - G\textbackslash):
\textbackslash{[}
M\textbackslash dot\{s\} + Cs = M\textbackslash ddot\{q\}\_r - M\textbackslash ddot\{q\} + Cs = M\textbackslash ddot\{q\}\_r + C\textbackslash dot\{q\} + G - \textbackslash tau + C(\textbackslash dot\{q\}\_r - \textbackslash dot\{q\})
= M\textbackslash ddot\{q\}\_r + C\textbackslash dot\{q\}\_r + G - \textbackslash tau
\textbackslash{]}
By linearity-in-parameters, \textbackslash(M\textbackslash ddot\{q\}\_r + C\textbackslash dot\{q\}\_r + G = Y\textbackslash theta\textbackslash). Substituting the control law \textbackslash(\textbackslash tau = Y\textbackslash hat\{\textbackslash theta\} + K\_D s\textbackslash):
\textbackslash{[}
M\textbackslash dot\{s\} + Cs = Y\textbackslash theta - Y\textbackslash hat\{\textbackslash theta\} - K\_D s = -Y(\textbackslash hat\{\textbackslash theta\} - \textbackslash theta) - K\_D s = -Y\textbackslash tilde\{\textbackslash theta\} - K\_D s
\textbackslash{]}

\textbf{Step 4: Substitute back into \textbackslash(\textbackslash dot\{V\}\textbackslash).}

\textbackslash{[}
\textbackslash dot\{V\} = s\^{}T(-Y\textbackslash tilde\{\textbackslash theta\} - K\_D s) + \textbackslash tilde\{\textbackslash theta\}\^{}T \textbackslash Gamma\textbackslash,\textbackslash dot\{\textbackslash tilde\{\textbackslash theta\}\}
= -s\^{}T Y\textbackslash tilde\{\textbackslash theta\} - s\^{}T K\_D s + \textbackslash tilde\{\textbackslash theta\}\^{}T \textbackslash Gamma\textbackslash,\textbackslash dot\{\textbackslash tilde\{\textbackslash theta\}\}
\textbackslash{]}

\textbf{Step 5: Cancel the cross term using the update law.}\\
Substitute \textbackslash(\textbackslash dot\{\textbackslash tilde\{\textbackslash theta\}\} = \textbackslash dot\{\textbackslash hat\{\textbackslash theta\}\} = \textbackslash Gamma\^{}\{-1\}Y\^{}T s\textbackslash):
\textbackslash{[}
\textbackslash tilde\{\textbackslash theta\}\^{}T \textbackslash Gamma\textbackslash,\textbackslash dot\{\textbackslash tilde\{\textbackslash theta\}\} = \textbackslash tilde\{\textbackslash theta\}\^{}T \textbackslash Gamma\textbackslash,(\textbackslash Gamma\^{}\{-1\}Y\^{}T s) = \textbackslash tilde\{\textbackslash theta\}\^{}T Y\^{}T s = (Y\textbackslash tilde\{\textbackslash theta\})\^{}T s = s\^{}T Y\textbackslash tilde\{\textbackslash theta\}
\textbackslash{]}
The cross terms cancel exactly:
\textbackslash{[}
\textbackslash dot\{V\} = -s\^{}T Y\textbackslash tilde\{\textbackslash theta\} - s\^{}T K\_D s + s\^{}T Y\textbackslash tilde\{\textbackslash theta\} = -s\^{}T K\_D s
\textbackslash{]}

\textbf{Result:}
\textbackslash{[}
\textbackslash boxed\{\textbackslash dot\{V\} = -s\^{}T K\_D\textbackslash, s \textbackslash leq 0\}
\textbackslash{]}
Since \textbackslash(K\_D \textbackslash succ 0\textbackslash), we have \textbackslash(\textbackslash dot\{V\} \textless{} 0\textbackslash) whenever \textbackslash(s \textbackslash neq 0\textbackslash), so \textbackslash(V\textbackslash) is non-increasing and bounded below by zero. By Barbalat\textquotesingle s lemma, \textbackslash(s(t) \textbackslash to 0\textbackslash) as \textbackslash(t \textbackslash to \textbackslash infty\textbackslash). Since \textbackslash(s = \textbackslash dot\{e\} + \textbackslash Lambda e\textbackslash) with \textbackslash(\textbackslash Lambda \textbackslash succ 0\textbackslash), this implies \textbackslash(e(t) \textbackslash to 0\textbackslash) and \textbackslash(\textbackslash dot\{e\}(t) \textbackslash to 0\textbackslash). \textbackslash(\textbackslash square\textbackslash)

\textbf{Sign convention.} We define \textbackslash(\textbackslash tilde\{\textbackslash theta\} = \textbackslash hat\{\textbackslash theta\} - \textbackslash theta\textbackslash), so \textbackslash(\textbackslash dot\{\textbackslash tilde\{\textbackslash theta\}\} = \textbackslash dot\{\textbackslash hat\{\textbackslash theta\}\}\textbackslash). The Lyapunov weight on the parameter error term is \textbackslash(\textbackslash Gamma\textbackslash) (not \textbackslash(\textbackslash Gamma\^{}\{-1\}\textbackslash)), which is why the update law \textbackslash(\textbackslash dot\{\textbackslash hat\{\textbackslash theta\}\} = \textbackslash Gamma\^{}\{-1\}Y\^{}T s\textbackslash) produces the exact cancellation \textbackslash(\textbackslash tilde\{\textbackslash theta\}\^{}T\textbackslash Gamma(\textbackslash Gamma\^{}\{-1\}Y\^{}T s) = s\^{}T Y\textbackslash tilde\{\textbackslash theta\}\textbackslash). Some textbooks define \textbackslash(\textbackslash tilde\{\textbackslash theta\} = \textbackslash theta - \textbackslash hat\{\textbackslash theta\}\textbackslash); with that reversed convention the update law carries a negative sign, but the underlying physics is identical.

\textbf{Result:}
\textbackslash{[}
\textbackslash boxed\{\textbackslash dot\{V\} = -s\^{}T K\_D\textbackslash, s \textbackslash leq 0\}
\textbackslash{]}
Since \textbackslash(K\_D \textbackslash succ 0\textbackslash), \textbackslash(\textbackslash dot\{V\} \textless{} 0\textbackslash) whenever \textbackslash(s \textbackslash neq 0\textbackslash). By Barbalat\textquotesingle s lemma, \textbackslash(s(t) \textbackslash to 0\textbackslash) as \textbackslash(t \textbackslash to \textbackslash infty\textbackslash). Since \textbackslash(s = \textbackslash dot\{e\} + \textbackslash Lambda e\textbackslash) and \textbackslash(\textbackslash Lambda \textbackslash succ 0\textbackslash), this implies \textbackslash(e(t) \textbackslash to 0\textbackslash) and \textbackslash(\textbackslash dot\{e\}(t) \textbackslash to 0\textbackslash).

\textbf{Sign convention note:} We use \textbackslash(\textbackslash tilde\{\textbackslash theta\} = \textbackslash hat\{\textbackslash theta\} - \textbackslash theta\textbackslash), so \textbackslash(\textbackslash dot\{\textbackslash tilde\{\textbackslash theta\}\} = \textbackslash dot\{\textbackslash hat\{\textbackslash theta\}\}\textbackslash). With the Lyapunov function \textbackslash(V = \textbackslash tfrac\{1\}\{2\}s\^{}T M s + \textbackslash tfrac\{1\}\{2\}\textbackslash tilde\{\textbackslash theta\}\^{}T\textbackslash Gamma\textbackslash tilde\{\textbackslash theta\}\textbackslash), the update law that cancels the cross term is:
\textbackslash{[}
\textbackslash dot\{\textbackslash hat\{\textbackslash theta\}\} = \textbackslash Gamma\^{}\{-1\}\textbackslash,Y\^{}T\textbackslash!s
\textbackslash{]}
Some textbooks define \textbackslash(\textbackslash tilde\{\textbackslash theta\} = \textbackslash theta - \textbackslash hat\{\textbackslash theta\}\textbackslash) (reversed). With that convention \textbackslash(\textbackslash dot\{\textbackslash tilde\{\textbackslash theta\}\} = -\textbackslash dot\{\textbackslash hat\{\textbackslash theta\}\}\textbackslash), and the update law is written as \textbackslash(\textbackslash dot\{\textbackslash hat\{\textbackslash theta\}\} = \textbackslash Gamma\^{}\{-1\} Y\^{}T s\textbackslash) with opposite sign in the cancellation. The physics is identical; only the bookkeeping differs. Always check which definition of \textbackslash(\textbackslash tilde\{\textbackslash theta\}\textbackslash) a textbook uses before comparing formulas.

\paragraph{Think-Pair-Share: Effect of Adaptation Gain}\label{think-pair-share-effect-of-adaptation-gain}}

Consider the adaptation gain matrix \textbackslash(\textbackslash Gamma\textbackslash). Discuss with your neighbour:

\begin{enumerate}
\tightlist
\item
  What happens if \textbackslash(\textbackslash Gamma\textbackslash) is very large (fast adaptation)?

  \begin{itemize}
  \tightlist
  \item
    Parameter estimates change rapidly, which can cause large transient oscillations and potentially excite unmodelled dynamics. In the worst case, the system becomes unstable in practice (even though the theory guarantees stability for the nominal model).
  \end{itemize}
\item
  What happens if \textbackslash(\textbackslash Gamma\textbackslash) is very small (slow adaptation)?

  \begin{itemize}
  \tightlist
  \item
    Parameters converge very slowly. The controller behaves essentially like a PD controller (the \textbackslash(K\_D s\textbackslash) term dominates) until the parameters have time to converge. Tracking errors may remain large for an extended period.
  \end{itemize}
\item
  Does the Lyapunov proof guarantee that \textbackslash(\textbackslash hat\{\textbackslash theta\} \textbackslash to \textbackslash theta\textbackslash)?

  \begin{itemize}
  \tightlist
  \item
    No. The proof only guarantees \textbackslash(s \textbackslash to 0\textbackslash) (and hence \textbackslash(e \textbackslash to 0\textbackslash)). Parameter convergence requires \emph{persistent excitation} of the regressor. If the trajectory does not excite all modes, some parameters may not converge to their true values, but tracking performance is still guaranteed.
  \end{itemize}
\end{enumerate}

\subsection{Section 4: Part 2 --- Adaptive Control for 2-DOF Manipulator}\label{section-4-part-2-adaptive-control-for-2-dof-manipulator}}

\subsubsection{4.1 System Setup}\label{system-setup}}

We now implement the full adaptive controller for the 2-DOF planar manipulator from Section 3.3. The true parameters are:

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}llll@{}}
\toprule\noalign{}
Parameter & Symbol & True Value & Initial Estimate \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
Link 1 mass & \textbackslash(m\_1\textbackslash) & 1.0 kg & 1.0 kg (known) \\
Link 2 mass & \textbackslash(m\_2\textbackslash) & 2.0 kg & 1.0 kg (50\% error) \\
Link 1 length & \textbackslash(l\_1\textbackslash) & 1.0 m & 1.0 m (known) \\
Link 2 length & \textbackslash(l\_2\textbackslash) & 1.0 m & 1.0 m (known) \\
Gravity & \textbackslash(g\textbackslash) & 9.81 m/s² & 9.81 m/s² (known) \\
\end{longtable}

Even though only \textbackslash(m\_2\textbackslash) is wrong, all composite parameters involving \textbackslash(m\_2\textbackslash) are affected:

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}lll@{}}
\toprule\noalign{}
Composite Parameter & True Value & Initial Estimate \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
\textbackslash(\textbackslash theta\_1 = m\_1 l\_1\^{}2\textbackslash) & 1.0 & 1.0 \\
\textbackslash(\textbackslash theta\_2 = m\_2 l\_1\^{}2\textbackslash) & 2.0 & 1.0 \\
\textbackslash(\textbackslash theta\_3 = m\_2 l\_2\^{}2\textbackslash) & 2.0 & 1.0 \\
\textbackslash(\textbackslash theta\_4 = m\_2 l\_1 l\_2\textbackslash) & 2.0 & 1.0 \\
\textbackslash(\textbackslash theta\_5 = (m\_1+m\_2)gl\_1\textbackslash) & 29.43 & 19.62 \\
\textbackslash(\textbackslash theta\_6 = m\_2 g l\_2\textbackslash) & 19.62 & 9.81 \\
\end{longtable}

\subsubsection{4.2 Building the Regressor Matrix}\label{building-the-regressor-matrix}}

We need the regressor evaluated at the reference quantities \textbackslash((\textbackslash dot\{q\}\_r, \textbackslash ddot\{q\}\_r)\textbackslash) instead of \textbackslash((\textbackslash dot\{q\}, \textbackslash ddot\{q\})\textbackslash). Define:

\textbackslash{[}
\textbackslash dot\{q\}\_r = \textbackslash dot\{q\}\_d + \textbackslash Lambda(q\_d - q), \textbackslash qquad \textbackslash ddot\{q\}\_r = \textbackslash ddot\{q\}\_d + \textbackslash Lambda(\textbackslash dot\{q\}\_d - \textbackslash dot\{q\})
\textbackslash{]}

The regressor \textbackslash(Y(q, \textbackslash dot\{q\}, \textbackslash dot\{q\}\_r, \textbackslash ddot\{q\}\_r) \textbackslash in \textbackslash mathbb\{R\}\^{}\{2 \textbackslash times 6\}\textbackslash) is constructed by replacing every occurrence of \textbackslash(\textbackslash ddot\{q\}\textbackslash) with \textbackslash(\textbackslash ddot\{q\}\_r\textbackslash) and every occurrence of \textbackslash(\textbackslash dot\{q\}\textbackslash) in the Coriolis terms with a mix of \textbackslash(\textbackslash dot\{q\}\textbackslash) and \textbackslash(\textbackslash dot\{q\}\_r\textbackslash) as appropriate. Specifically:

\textbackslash{[}
Y\_\{11\} = \textbackslash ddot\{q\}\_\{r1\}
\textbackslash{]}
\textbackslash{[}
Y\_\{12\} = \textbackslash ddot\{q\}\_\{r1\}
\textbackslash{]}
\textbackslash{[}
Y\_\{13\} = \textbackslash ddot\{q\}\_\{r1\} + \textbackslash ddot\{q\}\_\{r2\}
\textbackslash{]}
\textbackslash{[}
Y\_\{14\} = 2\textbackslash cos(q\_2)\textbackslash ddot\{q\}\_\{r1\} + \textbackslash cos(q\_2)\textbackslash ddot\{q\}\_\{r2\} - \textbackslash sin(q\_2)\textbackslash dot\{q\}\_2\textbackslash dot\{q\}\_\{r1\} - \textbackslash sin(q\_2)(\textbackslash dot\{q\}\_1 + \textbackslash dot\{q\}\_2)\textbackslash dot\{q\}\_\{r2\}
\textbackslash{]}
\textbackslash{[}
Y\_\{15\} = \textbackslash sin(q\_1)
\textbackslash{]}
\textbackslash{[}
Y\_\{16\} = \textbackslash sin(q\_1 + q\_2)
\textbackslash{]}
\textbackslash{[}
Y\_\{21\} = 0
\textbackslash{]}
\textbackslash{[}
Y\_\{22\} = 0
\textbackslash{]}
\textbackslash{[}
Y\_\{23\} = \textbackslash ddot\{q\}\_\{r1\} + \textbackslash ddot\{q\}\_\{r2\}
\textbackslash{]}
\textbackslash{[}
Y\_\{24\} = \textbackslash cos(q\_2)\textbackslash ddot\{q\}\_\{r1\} + \textbackslash sin(q\_2)\textbackslash dot\{q\}\_1 \textbackslash dot\{q\}\_\{r1\}
\textbackslash{]}
\textbackslash{[}
Y\_\{25\} = 0
\textbackslash{]}
\textbackslash{[}
Y\_\{26\} = \textbackslash sin(q\_1 + q\_2)
\textbackslash{]}

\subsubsection{4.3 Complete Control Algorithm}\label{complete-control-algorithm}}

Algorithm: Adaptive Control for 2-DOF Manipulator

\textbf{Given:} desired trajectory q\_d(t), dq\_d(t), ddq\_d(t)
\textbf{Initialise:} theta\_hat = theta\_hat\_0 (initial parameter estimates)
\textbf{Design parameters:} Lambda, K\_D, Gamma (all positive definite)
\textbf{At each time step:}
1. Measure q, dq
2. Compute errors:
e = q\_d - q
de = dq\_d - dq
3. Compute reference signals:
dq\_r = dq\_d + Lambda * e
ddq\_r = ddq\_d + Lambda * de
4. Compute sliding variable:
s = de + Lambda * e (equivalently s = dq\_r - dq)
5. Build regressor Y(q, dq, dq\_r, ddq\_r) {[}2x6 matrix{]}
6. Compute control torque:
tau = Y * theta\_hat + K\_D * s
7. Update parameter estimates:
d(theta\_hat)/dt = Gamma\_inv * Y\textquotesingle{} * s
8. Apply tau to the plant

\subsubsection{4.4 MATLAB Implementation}\label{matlab-implementation}}

\%\% Adaptive Control for 2-DOF Manipulator
\% True parameters
m1 = 1; m2 = 2; l1 = 1; l2 = 1; g = 9.81;
theta\_true = {[}m1*l1\^{}2; m2*l1\^{}2; m2*l2\^{}2; m2*l1*l2; (m1+m2)*g*l1; m2*g*l2{]};
\% Initial estimates (m2\_hat = 1 instead of 2)
m2\_hat = 1;
theta\_hat0 = {[}m1*l1\^{}2; m2\_hat*l1\^{}2; m2\_hat*l2\^{}2; m2\_hat*l1*l2; ...
(m1+m2\_hat)*g*l1; m2\_hat*g*l2{]};
\% Design parameters
Lambda = 10*eye(2); \% Sliding surface slope
K\_D = 50*eye(2); \% Damping gain
Gamma\_inv = 5*eye(6); \% Inverse adaptation gain
\% Desired trajectory
qd = @(t) {[}0.5*sin(t); 0.5*cos(t){]};
dqd = @(t) {[}0.5*cos(t); -0.5*sin(t){]};
ddqd = @(t) {[}-0.5*sin(t); -0.5*cos(t){]};
\% State: {[}q1; q2; dq1; dq2; theta\_hat(1:6){]}
x0 = {[}0; 0; 0; 0; theta\_hat0{]};
tspan = 0:0.01:20;
{[}t, X{]} = ode45(@(t,x) adaptive\_manipulator(t, x, ...
m1, m2, l1, l2, g, Lambda, K\_D, Gamma\_inv, qd, dqd, ddqd), tspan, x0);
function dx = adaptive\_manipulator(t, x, m1, m2, l1, l2, g, ...
Lambda, K\_D, Gamma\_inv, qd, dqd, ddqd)
q = x(1:2); dq = x(3:4); theta\_hat = x(5:10);
\% True dynamics matrices
c2 = cos(q(2)); s2 = sin(q(2));
M = {[}m1*l1\^{}2 + m2*(l1\^{}2+l2\^{}2+2*l1*l2*c2), m2*(l2\^{}2+l1*l2*c2);
m2*(l2\^{}2+l1*l2*c2), m2*l2\^{}2{]};
C = {[}-m2*l1*l2*s2*dq(2), -m2*l1*l2*s2*(dq(1)+dq(2));
m2*l1*l2*s2*dq(1), 0{]};
G = {[}(m1+m2)*g*l1*sin(q(1)) + m2*g*l2*sin(q(1)+q(2));
m2*g*l2*sin(q(1)+q(2)){]};
\% Errors and reference signals
e = qd(t) - q;
de = dqd(t) - dq;
dqr = dqd(t) + Lambda*e;
ddqr = ddqd(t) + Lambda*de;
s = de + Lambda*e;
\% Build regressor Y(q, dq, dqr, ddqr) {[}2x6{]}
s2q = sin(q(2)); c2q = cos(q(2));
s1 = sin(q(1)); s12 = sin(q(1)+q(2));
Y = zeros(2,6);
Y(1,1) = ddqr(1);
Y(1,2) = ddqr(1);
Y(1,3) = ddqr(1) + ddqr(2);
Y(1,4) = 2*c2q*ddqr(1) + c2q*ddqr(2) ...
- s2q*dq(2)*dqr(1) - s2q*(dq(1)+dq(2))*dqr(2);
Y(1,5) = s1;
Y(1,6) = s12;
Y(2,1) = 0;
Y(2,2) = 0;
Y(2,3) = ddqr(1) + ddqr(2);
Y(2,4) = c2q*ddqr(1) + s2q*dq(1)*dqr(1);
Y(2,5) = 0;
Y(2,6) = s12;
\% Adaptive control law
tau = Y*theta\_hat + K\_D*s;
\% Parameter update law
dtheta\_hat = Gamma\_inv * Y\textquotesingle{} * s;
\% True dynamics
ddq = M \textbackslash{} (tau - C*dq - G);
dx = {[}dq; ddq; dtheta\_hat{]};
end

\subsection{Section 5: Part 3 --- Adaptive Control for Mobile Robot}\label{section-5-part-3-adaptive-control-for-mobile-robot}}

\subsubsection{5.1 Parametric Uncertainty in Mobile Robots}\label{parametric-uncertainty-in-mobile-robots}}

\textbf{Figure 5:} Top-down view of a differential-drive mobile robot. The robot (blue) follows an actual path (pink) while tracking a reference trajectory (dashed purple). Tracking errors eₓ and eₙ are measured in the body frame. The uncertain parameters r (wheel radius) and b (track width) are indicated.

A differential-drive mobile robot converts wheel angular velocities \textbackslash((\textbackslash omega\_L, \textbackslash omega\_R)\textbackslash) to linear and angular velocity via:

\textbackslash{[}
v = \textbackslash frac\{r\}\{2\}(\textbackslash omega\_R + \textbackslash omega\_L), \textbackslash qquad \textbackslash omega = \textbackslash frac\{r\}\{b\}(\textbackslash omega\_R - \textbackslash omega\_L)
\textbackslash{]}

where \textbackslash(r\textbackslash) is the wheel radius and \textbackslash(b\textbackslash) is the track width (distance between wheels). The kinematic model in task space is:

\textbackslash{[}
\textbackslash begin\{bmatrix\} \textbackslash dot\{x\} \textbackslash\textbackslash{} \textbackslash dot\{y\} \textbackslash\textbackslash{} \textbackslash dot\{\textbackslash phi\} \textbackslash end\{bmatrix\}
= \textbackslash begin\{bmatrix\} \textbackslash cos\textbackslash phi \& 0 \textbackslash\textbackslash{} \textbackslash sin\textbackslash phi \& 0 \textbackslash\textbackslash{} 0 \& 1 \textbackslash end\{bmatrix\}
\textbackslash begin\{bmatrix\} v \textbackslash\textbackslash{} \textbackslash omega \textbackslash end\{bmatrix\}
\textbackslash{]}

Uncertainties arise from:

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  \textbf{Wheel radius \textbackslash(r\textbackslash):} Tire pressure variations, wear, and deformation under load change the effective radius.
\item
  \textbf{Track width \textbackslash(b\textbackslash):} Manufacturing tolerances and wheel alignment errors affect the effective distance between contact patches.
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection{5.2 Linearity in Parameters for the Kinematic Model}\label{linearity-in-parameters-for-the-kinematic-model}}

We can write the velocity-level kinematics in a form that is linear in the uncertain parameters. Define the control inputs as the wheel angular velocities \textbackslash(u = {[}\textbackslash omega\_R, \textbackslash omega\_L{]}\^{}T\textbackslash). Then:

\textbackslash{[}
\textbackslash begin\{bmatrix\} v \textbackslash\textbackslash{} \textbackslash omega \textbackslash end\{bmatrix\}
= \textbackslash begin\{bmatrix\} \textbackslash frac\{1\}\{2\}\textbackslash omega\_R + \textbackslash frac\{1\}\{2\}\textbackslash omega\_L \textbackslash\textbackslash{} \textbackslash frac\{1\}\{b\}(\textbackslash omega\_R - \textbackslash omega\_L) \textbackslash end\{bmatrix\}
\textbackslash cdot r
\textbackslash{]}

Define the parameter vector \textbackslash(\textbackslash theta\_m = {[}r,\textbackslash; r/b{]}\^{}T\textbackslash). Then:

\textbackslash{[}
\textbackslash begin\{bmatrix\} v \textbackslash\textbackslash{} \textbackslash omega \textbackslash end\{bmatrix\}
= \textbackslash underbrace\{\textbackslash begin\{bmatrix\}
\textbackslash frac\{1\}\{2\}(\textbackslash omega\_R + \textbackslash omega\_L) \& 0 \textbackslash\textbackslash{}
0 \& \textbackslash omega\_R - \textbackslash omega\_L
\textbackslash end\{bmatrix\}\}\_\{Y\_m(u)\}
\textbackslash begin\{bmatrix\} r \textbackslash\textbackslash{} r/b \textbackslash end\{bmatrix\}
\textbackslash{]}

\subsubsection{5.3 Adaptive Tracking Controller}\label{adaptive-tracking-controller}}

For trajectory tracking, we use a Lyapunov-based approach. Define the position error in the body frame:

\textbackslash{[}
\textbackslash begin\{bmatrix\} e\_x \textbackslash\textbackslash{} e\_y \textbackslash\textbackslash{} e\_\textbackslash phi \textbackslash end\{bmatrix\}
= \textbackslash begin\{bmatrix\} \textbackslash cos\textbackslash phi \& \textbackslash sin\textbackslash phi \& 0 \textbackslash\textbackslash{} -\textbackslash sin\textbackslash phi \& \textbackslash cos\textbackslash phi \& 0 \textbackslash\textbackslash{} 0 \& 0 \& 1 \textbackslash end\{bmatrix\}
\textbackslash begin\{bmatrix\} x\_d - x \textbackslash\textbackslash{} y\_d - y \textbackslash\textbackslash{} \textbackslash phi\_d - \textbackslash phi \textbackslash end\{bmatrix\}
\textbackslash{]}

The desired velocity commands are computed from a standard tracking law:

\textbackslash{[}
v\_c = v\_d \textbackslash cos(e\_\textbackslash phi) + k\_1 e\_x
\textbackslash{]}
\textbackslash{[}
\textbackslash omega\_c = \textbackslash omega\_d + v\_d(k\_2 e\_y + k\_3 \textbackslash sin(e\_\textbackslash phi))
\textbackslash{]}

where \textbackslash(v\_d, \textbackslash omega\_d\textbackslash) are the desired linear and angular velocities along the reference trajectory, and \textbackslash(k\_1, k\_2, k\_3 \textgreater{} 0\textbackslash) are gains.

With known \textbackslash(r\textbackslash) and \textbackslash(b\textbackslash), we would directly compute the wheel speeds. With unknown parameters, we use adaptive estimation. The actual velocities depend on uncertain \textbackslash(\textbackslash theta\_m\textbackslash):

\textbackslash{[}
\textbackslash begin\{bmatrix\} v \textbackslash\textbackslash{} \textbackslash omega \textbackslash end\{bmatrix\} = Y\_m(u)\textbackslash,\textbackslash hat\{\textbackslash theta\}\_m + \textbackslash text\{estimation error\}
\textbackslash{]}

The parameter update law is:

\textbackslash{[}
\textbackslash dot\{\textbackslash hat\{\textbackslash theta\}\}\_m = \textbackslash Gamma\_m\textbackslash, Y\_m\^{}T\textbackslash, \textbackslash begin\{bmatrix\} e\_x \textbackslash\textbackslash{} e\_\textbackslash phi \textbackslash end\{bmatrix\}
\textbackslash{]}

where \textbackslash(\textbackslash Gamma\_m \textbackslash succ 0\textbackslash) is the adaptation gain for the mobile robot parameters.

\subsubsection{5.4 MATLAB Implementation}\label{matlab-implementation-1}}

\%\% Adaptive Control for Differential-Drive Mobile Robot
\% True parameters
r\_true = 0.05; \% wheel radius {[}m{]}
b\_true = 0.3; \% track width {[}m{]}
theta\_m\_true = {[}r\_true; r\_true/b\_true{]};
\% Initial estimates (20\% error on both)
r\_hat0 = 0.04; b\_hat0 = 0.36;
theta\_m\_hat0 = {[}r\_hat0; r\_hat0/b\_hat0{]};
\% Tracking gains
k1 = 3; k2 = 5; k3 = 3;
Gamma\_m = diag({[}0.01, 0.05{]});
\% Circular reference trajectory
R\_circ = 2; omega\_circ = 0.5;
xd = @(t) R\_circ*cos(omega\_circ*t);
yd = @(t) R\_circ*sin(omega\_circ*t);
phid = @(t) omega\_circ*t + pi/2;
vd = @(t) R\_circ*omega\_circ;
wd = @(t) omega\_circ;
\% State: {[}x; y; phi; theta\_m\_hat(1:2){]}
x0\_mob = {[}R\_circ; 0; pi/2; theta\_m\_hat0{]};
tspan = 0:0.01:30;
{[}t\_mob, X\_mob{]} = ode45(@(t,x) adaptive\_mobile(t, x, ...
r\_true, b\_true, k1, k2, k3, Gamma\_m, ...
xd, yd, phid, vd, wd), tspan, x0\_mob);
function dx = adaptive\_mobile(t, x, r\_true, b\_true, ...
k1, k2, k3, Gamma\_m, ...
xd, yd, phid, vd, wd)
px = x(1); py = x(2); phi = x(3);
theta\_hat = x(4:5);
\% Body-frame errors
ex = cos(phi)*(xd(t)-px) + sin(phi)*(yd(t)-py);
ey = -sin(phi)*(xd(t)-px) + cos(phi)*(yd(t)-py);
ephi = phid(t) - phi;
ephi = atan2(sin(ephi), cos(ephi)); \% wrap to {[}-pi,pi{]}
\% Desired velocity commands
v\_c = vd(t)*cos(ephi) + k1*ex;
w\_c = wd(t) + vd(t)*(k2*ey + k3*sin(ephi));
\% Inverse kinematics using estimated parameters
r\_hat = theta\_hat(1);
rb\_hat = theta\_hat(2); \% r/b estimate
omega\_R = (v\_c/r\_hat) + (w\_c/(2*rb\_hat));
omega\_L = (v\_c/r\_hat) - (w\_c/(2*rb\_hat));
\% True velocities (using true parameters)
v\_actual = (r\_true/2)*(omega\_R + omega\_L);
w\_actual = (r\_true/b\_true)*(omega\_R - omega\_L);
\% Kinematics
dpx = v\_actual*cos(phi);
dpy = v\_actual*sin(phi);
dphi = w\_actual;
\% Parameter update
Y\_m = {[}0.5*(omega\_R+omega\_L), 0; 0, omega\_R-omega\_L{]};
e\_adapt = {[}ex; ephi{]};
dtheta = Gamma\_m * Y\_m\textquotesingle{} * e\_adapt;
dx = {[}dpx; dpy; dphi; dtheta{]};
end

\subsection{Section 6: Part 4 --- Adaptive Control for Quadrotor}\label{section-6-part-4-adaptive-control-for-quadrotor}}

\subsubsection{6.1 Quadrotor Dynamics with Unknown Parameters}\label{quadrotor-dynamics-with-unknown-parameters}}

\textbf{Figure 6:} Quadrotor UAV showing four rotors with alternating CW/CCW rotation, thrust vectors (green arrows), body frame axes (xᵇ, yᵇ, zᵇ), weight force mg, and Euler angles. Unknown parameters include mass m and moments of inertia.

The translational dynamics of a quadrotor are:

\textbackslash{[}
m\textbackslash ddot\{p\} = \textbackslash begin\{bmatrix\} 0 \textbackslash\textbackslash{} 0 \textbackslash\textbackslash{} -mg \textbackslash end\{bmatrix\} + R\textbackslash begin\{bmatrix\} 0 \textbackslash\textbackslash{} 0 \textbackslash\textbackslash{} T \textbackslash end\{bmatrix\}
\textbackslash{]}

where \textbackslash(m\textbackslash) is the mass, \textbackslash(p = {[}x,y,z{]}\^{}T\textbackslash) is position, \textbackslash(R \textbackslash in SO(3)\textbackslash) is the rotation matrix, and \textbackslash(T\textbackslash) is the total thrust. The rotational dynamics (simplified, Euler angles \textbackslash(\textbackslash phi, \textbackslash theta, \textbackslash psi\textbackslash)) are:

\textbackslash{[}
I\_\{xx\}\textbackslash ddot\{\textbackslash phi\} = \textbackslash tau\_\textbackslash phi, \textbackslash qquad I\_\{yy\}\textbackslash ddot\{\textbackslash theta\} = \textbackslash tau\_\textbackslash theta, \textbackslash qquad I\_\{zz\}\textbackslash ddot\{\textbackslash psi\} = \textbackslash tau\_\textbackslash psi
\textbackslash{]}

The unknown parameters are:

\textbackslash{[}
\textbackslash theta\_d = \textbackslash begin\{bmatrix\} m \textbackslash\textbackslash{} m\textbackslash,g \textbackslash\textbackslash{} I\_\{xx\} \textbackslash\textbackslash{} I\_\{yy\} \textbackslash\textbackslash{} I\_\{zz\} \textbackslash end\{bmatrix\}
\textbackslash{]}

\subsubsection{6.2 Linearity in Parameters}\label{linearity-in-parameters}}

The translational dynamics can be written as:

\textbackslash{[}
m\textbackslash ddot\{p\} + \textbackslash begin\{bmatrix\} 0 \textbackslash\textbackslash{} 0 \textbackslash\textbackslash{} mg \textbackslash end\{bmatrix\} = R\textbackslash begin\{bmatrix\} 0 \textbackslash\textbackslash{} 0 \textbackslash\textbackslash{} T \textbackslash end\{bmatrix\}
\textbackslash{]}

Define the position error \textbackslash(e\_p = p\_d - p\textbackslash), sliding variable \textbackslash(s\_p = \textbackslash dot\{e\}\_p + \textbackslash Lambda\_p e\_p\textbackslash), and reference acceleration \textbackslash(\textbackslash ddot\{p\}\_r = \textbackslash ddot\{p\}\_d + \textbackslash Lambda\_p \textbackslash dot\{e\}\_p\textbackslash). The desired thrust vector is:

\textbackslash{[}
F\_\{\textbackslash mathrm\{des\}\} = \textbackslash hat\{m\}\textbackslash,\textbackslash ddot\{p\}\_r + \textbackslash begin\{bmatrix\} 0 \textbackslash\textbackslash{} 0 \textbackslash\textbackslash{} \textbackslash hat\{m\}\textbackslash hat\{g\} \textbackslash end\{bmatrix\} + K\_\{D,p\}\textbackslash,s\_p
\textbackslash{]}

This is linear in \textbackslash({[}\textbackslash hat\{m\},\textbackslash; \textbackslash hat\{m\}\textbackslash hat\{g\}{]}\^{}T\textbackslash):

\textbackslash{[}
F\_\{\textbackslash mathrm\{des\}\} = Y\_p\textbackslash,\textbackslash hat\{\textbackslash theta\}\_p + K\_\{D,p\}\textbackslash,s\_p
\textbackslash{]}

where:

\textbackslash{[}
Y\_p = \textbackslash begin\{bmatrix\}
\textbackslash ddot\{p\}\_\{r,x\} \& 0 \textbackslash\textbackslash{}
\textbackslash ddot\{p\}\_\{r,y\} \& 0 \textbackslash\textbackslash{}
\textbackslash ddot\{p\}\_\{r,z\} \& 1
\textbackslash end\{bmatrix\}, \textbackslash qquad
\textbackslash hat\{\textbackslash theta\}\_p = \textbackslash begin\{bmatrix\} \textbackslash hat\{m\} \textbackslash\textbackslash{} \textbackslash hat\{m\}\textbackslash hat\{g\} \textbackslash end\{bmatrix\}
\textbackslash{]}

The total thrust magnitude is \textbackslash(T = \textbackslash\textbar F\_\{\textbackslash mathrm\{des\}\}\textbackslash\textbar\textbackslash), and the desired orientation is extracted from \textbackslash(F\_\{\textbackslash mathrm\{des\}\}\textbackslash).

\subsubsection{6.3 Attitude Adaptive Control}\label{attitude-adaptive-control}}

For the attitude loop, define the orientation error \textbackslash(e\_a = {[}\textbackslash phi\_d - \textbackslash phi,\textbackslash; \textbackslash theta\_d - \textbackslash theta\_0,\textbackslash; \textbackslash psi\_d - \textbackslash psi{]}\^{}T\textbackslash), the sliding variable \textbackslash(s\_a = \textbackslash dot\{e\}\_a + \textbackslash Lambda\_a e\_a\textbackslash), and the reference angular acceleration \textbackslash(\textbackslash ddot\{\textbackslash eta\}\_r = \textbackslash ddot\{\textbackslash eta\}\_d + \textbackslash Lambda\_a \textbackslash dot\{e\}\_a\textbackslash). The adaptive torque is:

\textbackslash{[}
\textbackslash tau\_a = Y\_a\textbackslash,\textbackslash hat\{\textbackslash theta\}\_a + K\_\{D,a\}\textbackslash,s\_a
\textbackslash{]}

where:

\textbackslash{[}
Y\_a = \textbackslash mathrm\{diag\}(\textbackslash ddot\{\textbackslash eta\}\_\{r,1\},\textbackslash; \textbackslash ddot\{\textbackslash eta\}\_\{r,2\},\textbackslash; \textbackslash ddot\{\textbackslash eta\}\_\{r,3\}), \textbackslash qquad
\textbackslash hat\{\textbackslash theta\}\_a = \textbackslash begin\{bmatrix\} \textbackslash hat\{I\}\_\{xx\} \textbackslash\textbackslash{} \textbackslash hat\{I\}\_\{yy\} \textbackslash\textbackslash{} \textbackslash hat\{I\}\_\{zz\} \textbackslash end\{bmatrix\}
\textbackslash{]}

\subsubsection{6.4 Update Laws}\label{update-laws}}

\textbackslash{[}
\textbackslash dot\{\textbackslash hat\{\textbackslash theta\}\}\_p = \textbackslash Gamma\_p\^{}\{-1\}\textbackslash,Y\_p\^{}T\textbackslash,s\_p, \textbackslash qquad
\textbackslash dot\{\textbackslash hat\{\textbackslash theta\}\}\_a = \textbackslash Gamma\_a\^{}\{-1\}\textbackslash,Y\_a\^{}T\textbackslash,s\_a
\textbackslash{]}

\subsubsection{6.5 MATLAB Implementation}\label{matlab-implementation-2}}

\%\% Adaptive Control for Quadrotor
\% True parameters
m\_true = 1.5; g\_val = 9.81;
Ixx\_true = 0.03; Iyy\_true = 0.03; Izz\_true = 0.06;
theta\_p\_true = {[}m\_true; m\_true*g\_val{]};
theta\_a\_true = {[}Ixx\_true; Iyy\_true; Izz\_true{]};
\% Initial estimates (mass off by 50\%, inertias off by 40\%)
m\_hat0 = 1.0; g\_hat = 9.81;
theta\_p\_hat0 = {[}m\_hat0; m\_hat0*g\_hat{]};
theta\_a\_hat0 = {[}0.05; 0.05; 0.10{]};
\% Gains
Lambda\_p = 2*eye(3); K\_Dp = 15*eye(3); Gamma\_p\_inv = 0.5*eye(2);
Lambda\_a = 5*eye(3); K\_Da = 8*eye(3); Gamma\_a\_inv = 0.1*eye(3);
\% State: {[}x,y,z, dx,dy,dz, phi,theta,psi, dphi,dtheta,dpsi, theta\_p\_hat(2), theta\_a\_hat(3){]}
x0\_quad = zeros(17,1);
x0\_quad(13:14) = theta\_p\_hat0;
x0\_quad(15:17) = theta\_a\_hat0;
\% Helical trajectory
pd = @(t) {[}2*cos(0.5*t); 2*sin(0.5*t); 0.2*t{]};
dpd = @(t) {[}-sin(0.5*t); cos(0.5*t); 0.2{]};
ddpd = @(t) {[}-0.5*cos(0.5*t); -0.5*sin(0.5*t); 0{]};
psid = @(t) 0; dpsid = @(t) 0; ddpsid = @(t) 0;
tspan = 0:0.01:30;
{[}t\_q, X\_q{]} = ode45(@(t,x) adaptive\_quad(t, x, m\_true, g\_val, ...
Ixx\_true, Iyy\_true, Izz\_true, Lambda\_p, K\_Dp, Gamma\_p\_inv, ...
Lambda\_a, K\_Da, Gamma\_a\_inv, pd, dpd, ddpd, psid, dpsid, ddpsid), ...
tspan, x0\_quad);

The full implementation, including the ODE function for the quadrotor adaptive controller, is provided in the companion MATLAB file \texttt{adaptive\_control\_simulation.m}.

\subsection{Section 7: Part 5 --- MATLAB Implementation \& Activities}\label{section-7-part-5-matlab-implementation-activities}}

\subsubsection{Activity 1: Parameter Convergence (10 minutes)}\label{activity-1-parameter-convergence-10-minutes}}

\paragraph{Instructions}\label{instructions}}

\begin{enumerate}
\tightlist
\item
  Open \texttt{adaptive\_control\_simulation.m} in MATLAB.
\item
  Run Section 1 (2-DOF Manipulator Adaptive Control). The true mass is \textbackslash(m\_2 = 2\textbackslash) kg, but the initial estimate is \textbackslash(\textbackslash hat\{m\}\_2 = 1\textbackslash) kg.
\item
  Observe the six subplots of parameter estimates \textbackslash(\textbackslash hat\{\textbackslash theta\}\_i(t)\textbackslash) versus the true values \textbackslash(\textbackslash theta\_i\textbackslash).
\item
  Answer: Do all parameters converge to their true values? If not, which ones do not, and why?
\end{enumerate}

\textbf{Expected observation:} Parameters that appear in the regressor columns excited by the sinusoidal trajectory will converge. Parameters associated with modes not excited may converge to incorrect values --- but tracking is still achieved. This is the difference between \emph{stability} (guaranteed by Lyapunov) and \emph{parameter convergence} (requires persistent excitation).

\subsubsection{Activity 2: Adaptive vs Computed Torque Under Uncertainty (15 minutes)}\label{activity-2-adaptive-vs-computed-torque-under-uncertainty-15-minutes}}

\paragraph{Instructions}\label{instructions-1}}

\begin{enumerate}
\tightlist
\item
  Run Section 4 of the MATLAB file, which compares:

  \begin{itemize}
  \tightlist
  \item
    \textbf{Computed torque} using incorrect parameters (\textbackslash(\textbackslash hat\{m\}\_2 = 1\textbackslash) kg, true \textbackslash(m\_2 = 2\textbackslash) kg).
  \item
    \textbf{Adaptive control} with the same initial parameter estimates.
  \end{itemize}
\item
  Compare the tracking error plots side-by-side.
\item
  Answer these questions:

  \begin{itemize}
  \tightlist
  \item
    What is the steady-state tracking error for computed torque with wrong parameters?
  \item
    How long does adaptive control take to reduce the error to below the computed torque error?
  \item
    Is there a transient period where adaptive control is actually worse? Why?
  \end{itemize}
\end{enumerate}

\subsubsection{Quiz}\label{quiz}}

\paragraph{Quick Quiz: Regressor Construction}\label{quick-quiz-regressor-construction}}

\textbf{Question:} Consider the gravity term for link 2 of the 2-DOF manipulator:

\textbackslash{[}
G\_2 = m\_2\textbackslash,g\textbackslash,l\_2\textbackslash,\textbackslash sin(q\_1 + q\_2)
\textbackslash{]}

This is the second element of the gravity vector \textbackslash(G(q)\textbackslash). Define a composite parameter \textbackslash(\textbackslash theta\_6 = m\_2 g l\_2\textbackslash). What is the corresponding regressor entry?

\textbf{Answer:} The regressor entry for the second row (joint 2 equation) and the column corresponding to \textbackslash(\textbackslash theta\_6\textbackslash) is simply:

\textbackslash{[}
Y\_\{2,6\} = \textbackslash sin(q\_1 + q\_2)
\textbackslash{]}

Because \textbackslash(G\_2 = \textbackslash theta\_6 \textbackslash cdot \textbackslash sin(q\_1 + q\_2)\textbackslash). The regressor separates the known kinematic function \textbackslash(\textbackslash sin(q\_1+q\_2)\textbackslash) from the unknown parameter \textbackslash(\textbackslash theta\_6 = m\_2 g l\_2\textbackslash).

\textbf{Follow-up:} Does the first row (joint 1 equation) also have a \textbackslash(\textbackslash theta\_6\textbackslash) entry? Yes:

\textbackslash{[}
Y\_\{1,6\} = \textbackslash sin(q\_1 + q\_2)
\textbackslash{]}

because the gravity contribution of link 2 to joint 1 is also \textbackslash(m\_2 g l\_2 \textbackslash sin(q\_1+q\_2)\textbackslash).

\subsection{Section 8: Summary --- Control Methods Comparison}\label{section-8-summary-control-methods-comparison}}

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}llll@{}}
\toprule\noalign{}
Property & Computed Torque (Week 1) & MPC (Week 2) & Adaptive Control (Week 3) \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
\textbf{Core idea} & Cancel nonlinearities with exact model, reduce to linear error dynamics & Predict future states over horizon, optimise control sequence online & Estimate unknown parameters online while controlling; Lyapunov guarantees stability \\
\textbf{Model requirement} & Exact knowledge of \textbackslash(M(q), C(q,\textbackslash dot\{q\}), G(q)\textbackslash) & Known (possibly linearised) model for prediction & Known structure (regressor \textbackslash(Y\textbackslash)), unknown parameter values (\textbackslash(\textbackslash theta\textbackslash)) \\
\textbf{Handles uncertainty?} & No --- performance degrades with parameter errors & Partially --- robust MPC variants exist but add conservatism & Yes --- explicitly designed for parametric uncertainty \\
\textbf{Handles constraints?} & No (must add saturation externally) & Yes --- naturally incorporates state and input constraints & Not directly (parameter projection can enforce bounds on \textbackslash(\textbackslash hat\{\textbackslash theta\}\textbackslash)) \\
\textbf{Computation} & Low --- one matrix inversion per step & High --- solves QP at each step & Low --- regressor multiply + parameter ODE integration \\
\textbf{Stability guarantee} & Exponential (with exact model) & Guaranteed with terminal cost/constraint & Asymptotic via Lyapunov + Barbalat\textquotesingle s lemma \\
\textbf{Parameter convergence} & N/A (parameters assumed known) & N/A (parameters assumed known) & Only with persistent excitation \\
\textbf{Key design parameters} & \textbackslash(K\_p, K\_v\textbackslash) (PD gains) & \textbackslash(Q, R, N\textbackslash) (cost weights, horizon) & \textbackslash(\textbackslash Lambda, K\_D, \textbackslash Gamma\textbackslash) (sliding surface, damping, adaptation rate) \\
\textbf{Best suited for} & Well-modelled systems with known parameters & Systems with constraints, preview information, and known models & Systems with unknown or slowly-varying parameters, no constraints \\
\end{longtable}

\textbf{Looking ahead --- Week 4: Robust Control.} What if the uncertainty is not parametric but includes unmodelled dynamics, disturbances, or unstructured uncertainty? Robust control (e.g., sliding mode control, \textbackslash(H\_\textbackslash infty\textbackslash)) provides guaranteed performance bounds without requiring online estimation. We will see how robust and adaptive approaches can be combined.
